We recommended that management require the:
Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical inventory of Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE) and Investment Property. This should include a reconciliation of the physical count with the property and accounting records, as required by Chapter 4 of the Handbook on Property & Supply Management System and Paragraph 4 of Section 114 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I. Any discrepancies should be thoroughly investigated, cleared, and reconciled immediately and written explanations should be obtained from accountable and responsible persons if necessary.
The procedures on one time cleansing of PPE account balances is recommended per COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020;
Accounting Office and General Services Office to enhance documentation and record-keeping practices related to PPE and Investment Property by preparing and continuously maintaining and updating PPE ledger cards and property cards, respectively. Both offices should ensure that all movements of PPE are properly recorded and documented to facilitate accurate reconciliation and reporting; and
Relevant members of the Inventory Committee to undergo training on proper inventory procedures, reconciliation processes, and record-keeping practices, if necessary.
We recommended that management require the observance of the following procedures:
conduct a thorough physical inventory of all PPEs to identify unserviceable items that need to be disposed of in accordance with the relevant laws and regulations;
once unserviceable items are identified, management should initiate the appropriate disposal procedures as prescribed by regulations. This may include destruction/condemnation, public auction, private/negotiated sale, or transfer to other government agencies;
after disposal, management should update the RPCPPE to reflect the removal of unserviceable items from the books; and
establish monitoring and review procedures to ensure that unserviceable items are promptly identified and disposed of in accordance with applicable laws and regulations.
We recommended that management require the observance of the following procedures:
the concerned personnel should immediately report missing PPE items to the Office of the Auditor by filing a notice of loss and applying for relief from property accountability and submitting available supporting evidence as required in Commission on Audit Memorandum No. 92-751. This should be done within the reglementary period of 30 days from the occurrence of the loss, as required by COA guidelines;
the GSO-designate should determine the employees responsible for the lost/missing items by examining its copies of Property Acknowledgement Receipts (PARs); and
there should be training and awareness programs to accountable officers and personnel involved in asset management to ensure understanding and compliance with reporting requirements for missing government property.

We recommended that management:
prioritize and accelerate the implementation of its ongoing projects funded by the 20% MDF to ensure that the allocated funds are utilized efficiently and effectively and ensure timely delivery of benefits to the community, such as improved infrastructure, services, and economic opportunities;
continuously align development projects funded by the 20% Development Fund (DF) with the municipality's development goals and the needs of its constituents. Projects should be selected based on their potential to contribute to efficient and effective local governance, as well as their ability to promote the general welfare of the people; and
ensure that the development projects to be funded out of the 20% DF are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready to avoid delays, cost overruns, and other issues that may arise during project execution.

We recommended that management implement the following measures:
Notice to Proceed:
Ensure that all Notice to Proceed documents include the date of receipt by the contractor. In cases where the date is missing, consider the date of the Notice to Proceed itself as the date of receipt.
Statement of Work Accomplished (SWA):
Implement a policy requiring the preparation of two SWAs for each project: one as of the supposed end of the contract duration and another as of the actual date of project completion. This will facilitate the accurate computation of liquidated damages against the contractor.
Preparation of SWA:
Establish a process where the SWA is prepared by the contractor and subsequently validated and/or confirmed by the procuring entity. This will ensure the accuracy and reliability of the SWA.
Certificate of Completion and Certificate of Acceptance:
Enforce the separation of the Certificate of Acceptance from the Certificate of Completion in project documentation. Additionally, ensure that the Certificate of Acceptance is issued no earlier than one year from the project's completion date.
Suspension of Work and Extension of Contract Time:
Strengthen oversight to ensure that all instances of work suspensions/extensions are supported by necessary documentary requirements. Non-compliance should result in the invalidation of the contract extension and the imposition of applicable liquidated damages in case of unjustified delays.
Non-imposition of Liquidated Damages:
Review all project contracts to ensure that liquidated damages are properly imposed for delays in project completion. Enforce the provisions of the contract agreements and the procurement law to hold contractors accountable for delays.
Submission of Procurement Documents:
Implement a system to track the submission of all procurement documents and documentary requirements on advance payment, progress payment, and final payment of infrastructure projects. Ensure that all required documents are submitted promptly to avoid delays in audit and inspection of projects.
Require personnel involved in procurement and project monitoring to undergo training and capacity-building activities to enhance their understanding of requirements and procedures.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to trace the original entry made, make the necessary adjustments on the outstanding payable accounts, and ensure that such claims are properly documented and supported with valid claims of speciﬁc creditors, processed vouchers or payrolls, demand payments or bills. The payable accounts that have been outstanding for more than two years and not supported with documents evidencing valid claims should be reverted to unappropriated surplus of the general fund.

We recommended the management to:
advise the concerned offices the release of the deposits made by suppliers/contractors in accordance with 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and continuously remind concerned claimants to submit their request for refund of their accounts;
in case of default, require the Accountant to record adjustment in the books; and
strictly comply with existing rules and regulations for accurate financial reporting.

We recommended the:
Municipal Accountant to perform analysis of the accounts with unremitted prior period balances and upon proper evaluation thereof, ensure that remittance be made promptly; and
ensure that all contributions and deductions be remitted on or before the scheduled deadline to avoid incurrence of penalties.

We recommended that management:
expedite the implementation of all on – going programmed and funded projects under the 20% MDF to optimize utilization thereof and to ensure timely delivery of intended benefits for the constituents of the municipality;
continuously ensure that the development projects that may be included under the 20% DF shall be those that are necessary, appropriate, or incidental to efficient and effective local governance, and those which are essential to the promotion of the general welfare of the people; and
ensure that the development projects to be funded out of the 20% DF are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready.

We recommended the management to:
conduct regular analysis on the causes of delay in the implementation of projects and to formulate specific, achievable and time-bound measures to ensure timely implementation of the projects;
continuously monitor and ensure that the transferred funds are used only for the purpose/s for which they were granted; and
request from the source agency the authority for its utilization for other purposes, if warranted.

We recommended management to make representations to the debtor agencies to enforce the collection of receivables especially those with sufficient information and demand liquidation for implemented projects in accordance with the Memorandum of Agreement, if any.

We recommended the management:
through the Municipal Accountant to conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the receivables and determine the concerned debtors to ascertain their collectability. If proven to be no longer collectible, request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts and attach required documents prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005; and
in the future, for transactions involving receivables, the Municipal Accountant should maintain subsidiary ledgers per Section 4 (e) of the NGAS to ensure complete information on the accounts and facilitate the collection thereof.

We recommended that management continuously enforce the settlement of the remaining disallowances and for the Municipal Accountant to properly monitor and reconcile the balance of receivables on disallowances/charges recognized in the financial statements with that contained in the RADC and make the necessary adjustments in consonance with the provisions set forth under COA Circular No. 2009-006.

observations.recommendation: We commended management for the efficient and full implementation of the identified programs, projects and activities funded by the Bayanihan Grant. Henceforth, we recommended that management sustain the same efforts in its future undertakings.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
in coordination with the BAC Chairman, submit immediately the lacking documentary requirements for verification; and
ensure that all documentary requirements prescribed by regulations are complied with before certifying as to completeness of the supporting documents of the disbursement vouchers.

We recommended that management ensure that all the relevant and required information in the Purchase Order are properly accomplished to ensure that the supplier will deliver the goods/items in accordance with the terms and conditions of the contract.
We recommended that management require the submission of the lacking supporting documents for further review by this office.

We recommended that management require:
the conduct of complete physical inventory of municipal properties and immediately reconcile the balances of any affected accounts to permit fair presentation of PPE in the financial statements;
the maintenance of PPE Subsidiary Ledger Cards and Property Cards to properly account the receipt and disposition of PPE items; and
the disclosures for the Land Account in the Notes to Financial Statements

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant thoroughly review the transactions which resulted in the negative balance of account and accordingly prepare adjustments to establish the accuracy and valuation thereof.

We recommended that management:
ensure that trust funds are used exclusively to the purpose/s for which they were granted following the provisions of Section 4(3) of P.D. No. 1445 and Sections 305(e) and 309(b) of R.A. No. 7160; and
require the settlement of long outstanding receivables to augment the deficient cash under trust fund.

We recommended that management:
require/demand the immediate settlement of all long overdue cash advances and if necessary, withhold the salaries of the concerned officials and employees for the settlement thereof; and
strictly comply with the provisions of Section 89 of P.D. No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97 – 002 dated February 10, 1997 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the receivables and determine the concerned debtors to ascertain their collectability. If proven to be no longer collectible, request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts and attach required documents prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005; and
maintain subsidiary ledgers in accordance with Section 4 (e) of the NGAS to ensure complete information on the accounts and to facilitate the collection thereof.

We recommended the following:
the Local Chief Executive and concerned municipal officials must ensure that at least 5% of the annual budget of the municipality can be attributed and utilized on GAD-related programs, projects and activities;
the members of the GAD Focal Point System to undergo trainings on HGDG to strengthen their knowledge and capabilities in the conduct of gender analysis of programs and projects for purposes of GAD budget attribution; and
the members of the GAD Focal Point System to use monitoring tools such as the HGDG and PIMME in the GAD planning and budgeting as well as in the preparation of the accomplishment report.

We recommended that management:
determine the accountable person/s on the loss of breeding stocks and require the Municipal Accountant to make an adjusting entry to set-up the corresponding receivable account from the accountable officer;
direct the accountable officer to file a relief of accountability on the loss of breeding stocks to the COA auditor; and
adhere with the procedures on accounting for the loss of properties as prescribed by Section 268 of the GAM, Volume I.
We recommended that management adhere with the procedures on accounting for the derecognition of PPE as prescribed by Section 267 of the GAM, Volume I.

We recommended that management:
revisit the Memorandum of Agreement on the construction of SLF if certain provision/s are violated which caused the delay in the implementation and file appropriate legal action, if any;
fast tract the implementation of SLF; and
sustain its efforts and commitment towards full compliance with standards and requirements set under R.A. No. 9003. To this end, sufficient appropriation should be provided to ensure its full and immediate implementation.

We recommended that management:
ensure the allocation and utilization of LDRRMF pursuant to the policies and guidelines primarily embodied in NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1; and
thru the LDRRMC, continuously monitor, coordinate and evaluate PPAs to ensure the completion of most urgent, needed, appropriate and eligible projects.

We recommended that the unexpended balance of ₱897,420.38 from CYs 2011 to 2014 LDRRMF appropriations, which are currently maintained in the Special Trust Fund, should be reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund for subsequent re-appropriation by the municipality for other social services.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
prepare necessary adjusting entry to the affected accounts to ensure fair presentation of financial statements;
cause the transfer of unexpended balance of DRRMF other than those appropriated for capital outlays to the Trust Fund, and record the same as Trust Liabilities – DRRMF; and
abide with the guidelines set forth under Items 5.1.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommended the following:
the Local Chief Executive to enforce the settlement of the above audit disallowances which have become final and executory and in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006; and
the Municipal Treasurer to immediately notify the Audit Team regarding settlement of disallowances and submit proof of settlement thereof for prompt issuance of NSSDC.

We recommended that management:
expedite the implementation of all on – going programmed and funded projects under the 20% MDF to optimize utilization thereof and to ensure timely delivery of intended benefits for the constituents of the municipality;
continuously monitor, coordinate, and evaluate PPAs to ensure the completion of most urgent, needed, appropriate and eligible development projects; and
observe the policies and guidelines in the appropriation and utilization of the 20% DF as enjoined by DBM-DOF-DILG-JMC No. 1, s. 2020

We recommended that the Management require the Disbursing Officer and the Accountant to reconcile their cashbook and accounting records, respectively, at least quarterly in compliance to Section 96 of the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the Accountable Officer:
explain in writing the incurrence of cash overage, otherwise, the amount shall be forfeited in favor of the government; and
properly monitor her accountabilities and observe proper safekeeping of collections by separating personal monies from the municipal funds and comply with the provision of Section 101(1) of P.D. No. 1445.
We recommended that the liquidating officer maintain/retain a permanent file of RCDs of collectors to facilitate the verification and reconciliation of individual accountabilities.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer require all collectors to render their RAAF at the end of each month. Henceforth, these individual RAAFs should be reconciled with the Consolidated RAAF to ensure its accuracy.

We recommended that management instruct the Municipal Accountant and Liquidating Officer to require the conduct of monthly reconciliation of cashbook and accounting records to ensure timely discovery of errors and to facilitate early preparation of applicable adjustments.

We recommended that management continue the practice of depositing collections regularly to comply with the rules and regulations as mandated by Section 69 of P.D. No. 1445 and Section 32 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I on collections and deposits.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management ensure the implementation of all programmed projects during each budget year to optimize the utilization of the 20% development fund as mandated under DILG-DBM Joint Circular No. 2011-1.

We recommended that management through the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office:
Monitor the utilization of LDRRMF focusing on programs, projects and activities (PPAs) requiring the highest priority in terms of urgency and/or impact on the four thematic areas of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management; and
Ensure efficient and effective implementation of all programmed projects identified during the budget year to optimize the utilization of the fund and to warrant that goals and objectives of reducing disaster risk and enhancing disaster preparedness and response capabilities be achieved.

We recommended that Management:
Prudently evaluate all projects and activities charged against the LDRRMF to comply with the NDRRMP and NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01;
Ensure utilization of the fund allocated for trainings; and
Consider the inclusion in the priority programs of training sessions to increase the level of awareness of the community to threats and impacts of all hazards and to equip the community with the necessary skills to cope with the negative impacts of a disaster.

We recommended that management only grant allowances and benefits authorized under the salary standardization law. Other allowances and benefits not included in the aforementioned enumeration may only be granted with legal basis.

We recommended that Management require the attachment of warranty certificates and the document indicating the history of repair on disbursements for repairs and maintenance of equipments, vehicles and others of similar nature as required under Section 9.1.3.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with the provisions of Revenue Memorandum Order No. 23-2014 on the withholding of taxes to avoid legal action from the Bureau of Internal Revenue against the erring withholding agent.

We recommended that Management reinforce efforts on the collection of audit disallowances as required under Sections 5.4 and 7.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006 and necessary documentations on the actions of the Municipality must be compiled, copy furnish the Office of the Auditor.

We recommended that the Municipality stop the practice of allowing reimbursement to officials and employees when direct payment is possible to prevent losses and provide reasonable assurance that the transaction is regular.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, together with the Municipal Treasurer, reconcile the discrepancy/difference noted between the balances of cash in bank  per book and per bank of the Municipality as at December 31, 2016 totaling P486,789.53  and effect the necessary adjustments, if any. The result of the reconciliation should be duly documented and a copy of which furnished to the Office of the Auditor for evaluation.

We recommended that Management require complete documentation of the financial assistance granted to NGOs/POs totaling P 248,000.00, and their actual fund utilization as prescribed under Section 2.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001. Further, we recommended the strict monitoring of the released funds, though already disbursed, these still retain their nature as public funds.

We recommended that Management:
Strictly comply with the provisions of Section 1 of the Republic Act No. 5447; and
Prudently evaluate all projects and activities to be undertaken if these are covered by the Special Education Fund before charging these to the Fund to avoid audit disallowances.

We recommended the Management to:
Strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-003 on the prevention of irregular, unnecessary, excessive, or extravagant expenditures of funds or uses of property; and
Take full cognizance of the Municipality’s primary responsibility and accountability to uphold prudent utilization of government funds for the welfare of its constituents.
We recommended that Management provide the Office of the Auditor the legal basis on the granting of postpaid plans to the provincial legal officers.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to analyze the components of the Registry of Public Infrastructures (RPI) for all prior years’ infrastructure assets and work on the gradual recognition of such into their corresponding PPE accounts in the financial statements to meet the target set under Section 3.7 of COA Circular No. 2016-004.

We recommended that Management:
Revisit the Municipality’s 10-Year SWM Plan. This shall be reviewed and updated every year by municipal solid waste management board as provided under Section 16 of Republic Act No.  9003; and
Implement the Municipality’s 10-Year SWM Plan, the identified issues in this report should be properly addressed.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive consider the execution of a contract of lease between the Municipality and the NUVELCO to avail of the benefits due to the Municipality as the owner of the property being occupied.

We recommended that Agency Head require the Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to promptly submit disbursement vouchers, trial balances and bank reconciliation statements to enable a timely and more effective review of the Agency’s financial transactions and evaluation of its operations.

We recommended that the Municipality strictly comply with the provisions of Section 93 of the General Provisions of Republic Act No. 10651, otherwise known as the General Appropriations Act of 2015, on the submission of AAPSI report to facilitate timely validation on the actions taken by the LGU on COA findings and recommendations.

We recommended that Management require all concerned officials to comply with the monthly submission of Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF using the format prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-002.

The Audit Team recommended that management do the following:

Conduct physical count of municipal properties and submit the resulting Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) as of December 31, 2015 to the Office of the Auditor; and

Reconcile the counted municipal properties with the subsidiary ledgers in order that the financial statements reflect the true value of existing municipal properties at hand as of year-end.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality conducted physical count of PPE for CY 2016. The resulting report was submitted to the Office of the Auditor on March 6, 2017.

observations.recommendation:
The Audit Team recommended that the accountable officers do the following:
Collect immediately the IOU’s given to the officers and employees of the LGU to settle the shortages discovered during the cash examination.

Adhere strictly to the provisions of Section 4(6) of PD 1445 that all claims should be supported with complete documentation to warrant the authority and validity of all claims being disbursed. The AOs should refrain from using IOUs to support payments out of government funds.
observations.managementAction: The Management already collected some of the IOUs disallowed during the cash examination. For the balance, the Municipality is on the process of collecting remaining IOUs from concerned officials.
